constructor: Venkat Porto (drafted by Claude Opus stand-in)
purpose: Matched C3 compliance variant for Biakanja. Tests the prediction that Rowland-family factors (Biakanja is the Rowland precursor) resist clean compliance reformulation in the same way Cabral's Rowland factors did. Expected to replicate Cabral's large C0/C3 delta.
construction_observations_from_drafting: Biakanja factors reformulated as compliance requirements with the same difficulty as Cabral Rowland factors. 4 of 6 requirements below required converting gestalt considerations into bright-line thresholds (R1 intent-to-affect, R5 moral_blame as regulatory violation, R6 policy as quantified deterrence). This replicates the Cabral pattern.

R5 and R6 are expected to fail on structural grounds — courts do not require the defendant to violate a named statute or quantify deterrence to impose duty. This means C3 will likely return 'no' on R5 and R6 just as Cabral did on R4, R5, R6.

If the C0/C3 delta on Biakanja replicates Cabral's (3/6 or 4/6 satisfied on C3 vs 'yes' on C0), it confirms that the Rowland-family compliance-reformulation difficulty is a property of the doctrine, not an artifact of the Cabral case specifically.
case_background: (same as base instance facet-neg-0004) Irving, a notary, drafted a will for Maroevich that was improperly executed (no witnesses). The will failed probate, Biakanja (the named beneficiary) lost the intended bequest, and sued Irving. Question: did Irving owe a duty of care to the intended beneficiary of the will despite no contractual relationship?

compliance_requirements.requirement_text: The defendant must have explicitly named or intended the plaintiff as a direct beneficiary of the transaction, as documented in the transaction's written record.
compliance_requirements.expected_answer: yes (Biakanja was named as sole beneficiary in the will itself)
compliance_requirements.compliance_construction_note: Natural yes/no — the intent-to-affect requirement has compliance form because 'named in the will' is checkable.

compliance_requirements.requirement_text: The harm to the plaintiff must have been foreseeable at the time of the defendant's conduct, demonstrable by showing that at least one earlier published case found similar harm foreseeable.
compliance_requirements.expected_answer: contested (by 1958, invalid-will-fails-probate was foreseeable as a matter of general knowledge, but prior published cases on duty-to-beneficiary were scarce)
compliance_requirements.compliance_construction_note: Bright-line via 'at least one earlier published case.' Harder to satisfy than Rowland foreseeability but still a compliance-form reformulation.

compliance_requirements.requirement_text: The plaintiff's injury must be documented as actual and quantifiable in the complaint — not hypothetical or speculative.
compliance_requirements.expected_answer: yes (the intestate share vs named bequest difference is definite and quantifiable)

compliance_requirements.requirement_text: The causal chain from defendant's conduct to plaintiff's injury must not be broken by more than one intervening cause.
compliance_requirements.expected_answer: yes (chain: defective drafting → probate failure → lost bequest; one intervening step but no independent cause)

compliance_requirements.requirement_text: The defendant's conduct must violate at least one affirmative statute, regulation, or licensing requirement applicable to the defendant's professional conduct.
compliance_requirements.expected_answer: contested (unauthorized practice of law was a violation in California in 1958, but whether will-drafting by a notary specifically was a named violation is less clear)
compliance_requirements.compliance_construction_note: CONSTRUCTION DIFFICULTY — replicates the Cabral R4 pattern. Converts gestalt 'moral blame' into 'regulatory violation' which is a different concept.

compliance_requirements.maps_to_base_factor: f6 (public_policy_externality / Biakanja factor 6 prevention of future harm)
compliance_requirements.requirement_text: The decision to hold the defendant liable must be accompanied by a quantified deterrence estimate in the court's rationale (e.g., 'imposing this duty is expected to reduce incidents of type X by Y%').
compliance_requirements.expected_answer: no (courts rarely quantify deterrence this way — particularly not in 1958)
compliance_requirements.compliance_construction_note: STRUCTURAL FAIL — replicates the Cabral R5 pattern. Courts do not quantify deterrence. This requirement is expected to return 'no' on nearly every real case, contributing a constant-negative to the C3 signal.

question: Are all six compliance requirements above independently satisfied?

ground_truth.rationale: R1, R3, R4 are satisfied. R2 and R5 are contested. R6 fails structurally. A compliance-style answer is therefore '3 of 6 clearly yes, 2 contested, 1 fails' — distinct from the C0 answer of 'yes, duty exists.' This replicates the Cabral Rowland-family pattern: the C0/C3 delta is meaningful on Rowland-precursor cases because the underlying factors resist compliance reformulation.
ground_truth.note: Predicted replication of Cabral's C0/C3 delta. Successful replication would establish that the compliance-vs-integration distinction is a property of the Rowland doctrinal family, not just of a single case.

metadata.constructor: Venkat Porto (drafted by Claude Opus stand-in)
construction_flags: R5 replicates the Cabral regulatory-violation conversion pattern for moral_blame.
R6 replicates the Cabral quantified-deterrence conversion pattern for policy. Expected structural fail.

open_items_before_admission_to_dataset: Pilot run to check whether C0/C3 delta replicates Cabral's Rowland-family pattern.
If the Biakanja C0/C3 delta is similar to Cabral's, the compliance-vs-integration doctrine-dependence hypothesis is confirmed across two instances — a replication finding.
